attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for routine COPD review. She reports increasing breathlessness over the past six months and has had two exacerbations requiring oral corticosteroids in the past year. She experiences breathlessness walking on flat ground and must stop after 100 metres. She has a medical history of COPD diagnosed five years ago and osteoporosis. Her current medications include a long-acting beta-agonist (LABA) inhaler used twice daily, salbutamol as needed, and calcium with vitamin D. She quit smoking three years ago after a 40 pack-year history.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 132/82 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 94% on room air, and temperature 36.6°C. Chest auscultation reveals decreased air entry bilaterally with scattered expiratory wheeze.

Recent investigations show:

  • FEV1: 1.2 L (45% predicted)
  • FEV1/FVC ratio: 52%
  • Full blood count: eosinophils 320 cells/μL (40-400)
  • Arterial blood gas (room air): pH 7.39 (7.35-7.45), PaO2 68 mmHg (75-100), PaCO2 42 mmHg (35-45), HCO3 25 mmol/L (22-28)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Add long-acting muscarinic antagonist (LAMA) to current therapy
attributes.explanation:
This patient with established COPD presents with worsening symptoms and two moderate exacerbations in the past year despite LABA monotherapy. Her FEV1 of 45% predicted indicates severe airflow limitation. The blood eosinophil count of 320 cells/μL is above the threshold that predicts a good response to inhaled corticosteroids. The arterial blood gas shows mild hypoxaemia without hypercapnia or acidosis.

Change to triple therapy inhaler (LABA/LAMA/ICS) is the most appropriate next step. The Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease (GOLD) guidelines have evolved significantly regarding the role of blood eosinophils in guiding ICS therapy. Current GOLD recommendations use eosinophil counts as a continuous variable rather than a simple cut-off, with increasing likelihood of ICS benefit as counts rise. At ≥100 cells/μL, there is a clinically relevant reduction in exacerbations with ICS therapy, and this benefit becomes more pronounced at ≥300 cells/μL, as seen in this patient. The guidelines specifically recommend considering ICS in patients with a history of exacerbations despite appropriate bronchodilator therapy when eosinophils are ≥100 cells/μL. With this patient's count of 320 cells/μL and two exacerbations in the past year, she has a strong indication for ICS addition. While the traditional stepwise approach would suggest adding a LAMA first to create dual bronchodilator therapy, the combination of frequent exacerbations and high eosinophil count supports moving directly to triple therapy to prevent further exacerbations.

Add long-acting muscarinic antagonist (LAMA) to current therapy would create dual bronchodilator therapy, which is typically the next step from LABA monotherapy. However, GOLD guidelines recognise that patients with exacerbation history and eosinophil counts ≥300 cells/μL derive substantial benefit from ICS therapy. Adding only a LAMA would likely result in continued exacerbations, necessitating escalation to triple therapy soon after.

Add azithromycin prophylaxis can reduce exacerbation frequency but is typically considered after optimising inhaled therapy including ICS. It requires baseline ECG to exclude QT prolongation and monitoring for hearing loss and bacterial resistance. GOLD guidelines position azithromycin as an option for patients who continue to exacerbate despite triple therapy.

Add long-term oxygen therapy is indicated for patients with resting hypoxaemia (PaO2 ≤55 mmHg or SpO2 ≤88%) or PaO2 56-59 mmHg with evidence of cor pulmonale. This patient's PaO2 of 68 mmHg and SpO2 of 94% do not meet criteria for long-term oxygen therapy.

Switch to nebulised bronchodilators offers no advantage over metered-dose or dry powder inhalers in stable COPD and is generally reserved for acute exacerbations or patients unable to use handheld devices effectively.

It is important to note that GOLD guidelines also consider eosinophil counts <100 cells/μL as a relative contraindication to ICS therapy due to increased pneumonia risk with minimal benefit. Conversely, counts ≥300 cells/μL, as in this case, identify patients who are not only more likely to benefit from ICS but also at higher risk of exacerbations if ICS is withdrawn. The guidelines emphasise that eosinophil-guided therapy represents precision medicine in COPD, allowing clinicians to identify patients most likely to benefit from ICS while avoiding unnecessary exposure in those unlikely to respond. Regular monitoring for ICS-related adverse effects, particularly pneumonia and oral candidiasis, remains important even in patients with favourable eosinophil profiles.
learningPoints: Triple therapy (LABA/LAMA/ICS) is indicated for COPD patients with frequent exacerbations despite dual bronchodilator therapy.
Two or more moderate exacerbations or one severe exacerbation per year despite optimal therapy warrants treatment escalation.
Blood eosinophil count ≥100 cells/μL can predict response to inhaled corticosteroids in COPD, with greater benefit at higher counts.
references: Yang IA, George J, McDonald CF, McDonald V, Ordman R, Goodwin A, Smith B, McNamara R, Zwar N, Dabscheck E. The COPD-X Plan: Australian and New Zealand Guidelines for the management of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease 2023. Version 2.73, December 2023.
Hopkinson NS, Molyneux A, Pink J, Harrisingh MC. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease: diagnosis and management: summary of updated NICE guidance. Bmj. 2019 Jul 29;366.

Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease. Global Strategy for the Diagnosis, Management, and Prevention of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease: 2025 Report. Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease; 2024. Available at: https://goldcopd.org/2025-gold-report/

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
